narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 61% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for admission. This prediction is based on your profile: a Year 1 school decile of 3, Year 3 decile of 2, middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 27, undergraduate GPA of 3.60, being female, White, and planning full-time enrollment. Compared to typical students, your LSAT is notably below the average of about 37, your early and later school deciles are lower than the usual mid-range around 5-6, while your undergrad GPA stands out as stronger than the typical 3.21. The model weighed your LSAT as the most influential factor, as your lower-than-average score strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Next in importance was your Year 3 decile, where being in the lower ranks compared to peers nudged the odds further toward failure. Your Year 1 decile followed closely, similarly contributing to that concerning direction because it was below what's common. Family income in the middle bracket played a moderate role, also tilting things negatively relative to the dataset. Even your solid undergrad GPA, while a positive on paper, had a smaller influence that still aligned with the failure prediction in the model's eyes. Less impactful were your race, gender, and full-time plans, which had minimal sway either way. Overall, these academic markers, led by your LSAT and school deciles, outweighed other aspects for this outcome. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above average, strengthen your study habits to improve performance in advanced coursework like Year 3, and build dedicated bar exam prep strategies early, such as practice tests and tutoring. Many students turn things around with targeted effort—keep pursuing your goals, and you can shift this prediction in your favor.
